展望纽约市大流行中期的景象，她看到了一个对六年级学生来说令人毛骨悚然的地方。她的女儿索菲娅(Sophia)刚刚从曼哈顿下城的那种公立学校毕业，在那里，PTA可以很容易地为iPad和智能板筹集资金。杰克逊喜欢索菲亚的日子安排得井井有条，也喜欢她能结交几个亲密的朋友。然而，在过去的六个月里，杰克逊对学校可能会如何影响她的孩子保持警惕。首先，索菲亚不喜欢戴面具。杰克逊告诉我：“如果我们去全食超市，她会穿上它，但她只是觉得自己受到了很大的限制。”在家里，没有必要设置这样的障碍：“她可以穿着内衣学习。”
杰克逊已经开始注意到在她位于翠贝卡的公寓附近的公园里有更多的无人居住的人。身为白人的杰克逊说，这座城市“现在总体上对我来说感觉不太安全”，他提到了“骚乱、喧闹和随机发生的暴力行为”。(根据“纽约时报”最近的一篇报道，虽然今年夏天纽约的枪击案激增，但总体犯罪率持平，远远低于20世纪80年代和90年代的高犯罪率。)。随着抗议者要求政府取消对警察的资助，她觉得这座城市似乎“保护得更少了”。由于家庭面临经济困难，城市处于紧张状态，杰克逊担心索菲亚可能会面临危险。她说：“你不知道教室里是否有一个孩子的父母正在经历一段艰难的时期，也许那个孩子会表现得很暴躁，然后决定打另一个孩子。”当新冠肺炎的疫苗最终到达时，杰克逊担心纽约官员会强制要求孩子们在公立学校接种疫苗的证明。“我对没有经过严格测试的疫苗感到不舒服，”杰克逊说。“让她暴露在如此可疑的事情中似乎不是一个明智的决定。”在大流行之前，她从来不想在家教育女儿。现在，她说，这似乎是她最好的选择。
新冠肺炎在美国教育界创造了一个奇怪的自然实验：原本从来不会考虑让孩子辍学的家庭，会感到非常绝望，想要尝试一下。重新开学一直很混乱：在纽约市，开学时间被推迟到9月下旬，因为教师和校长们争先恐后地为在线学习和面对面学习分开的学期做准备，努力确保安全地让孩子们重返课堂所需的额外人员配备和测试。
家庭教育组织和顾问面临着大批惊慌失措的家长，他们疯狂地寻找正规学校的替代方案。有些家庭讨厌他们的孩子一次在Zoom上坐上几个小时。其他人担心家庭成员暴露在冠状病毒下，或者在病例激增后看到学校突然关闭。尽管一旦疫情得到控制，其中一些家长可能会让他们的孩子重返学校，但在家上学的倡导者认为，这段时间不太可能是传播他们的生活方式的机会，他们称这种生活方式比传统学校更灵活、更有创造力，更适合每个学生。2016年，在家上学的家庭包括大约3%的美国学龄儿童，他们有很多不同的理由想要教育自己的孩子。但他们在一个共同的评估上是一致的：他们想要摆脱传统体系。问题是，随着家长们拼凑起疫情期间的日子，新冠肺炎是否会导致在家上学的人数暂时增加，还是标志着教育的一个永久性拐点，这种拐点在病毒得到控制后很长一段时间内仍在继续。一些家庭可能会发现他们想永远退出这个系统。
春季，索菲亚奋力度过了学年末，在一场快速协调的Zoom仪式中从小学毕业。但杰克逊对再拼凑一个学期的想法并不满意，所以整个夏天，她开始研究秋季的其他选择。她找到了乔安娜·艾伦·罗丁(Joanna Allen Lodin)，她是许多以前在家上学的母亲中的一员，她们为有意离开传统学校体系的家庭开设了小圣人商店。Lodin告诉我，自5月以来，她收到了要求提供信息的“滚雪球”，每周都会为好奇的家庭举办一到两次信息交流会议。
“我听到了各种各样的声音，从认为今年春天对孩子来说是一场灾难的父母，他们觉得自己可以做得更好，”她说，还有“那些说今年春天很有启发性的人”，他们喜欢让孩子呆在家里，他们得出的结论是，“学校只是照看孩子，我可以做得更好，现在我要在家里教育他们。”她说，共同的主线是“父母害怕辜负他们的孩子”。
今年秋天，各种各样的父母都试图在家上学，有经验的家庭也在努力帮助他们。克里斯汀·罗兹(Kristen Rhodes)，前公共科学委员会成员
问题是，在大流行的混乱中，还不清楚任何一种学校都能带来多少共同的好处。佐治亚大学研究黑人在家上学的副教授谢丽尔·菲尔兹-史密斯(Cheryl Fields-Smith)告诉我，在远程和面对面学习的混合模式下，最有可能受到影响的是那些无法上网或父母不得不长时间外出工作的孩子。这些孩子可能几乎没有其他选择--无论今年秋天情况变得多么糟糕，他们很可能会被困在传统的学校里，而拥有更多资源的父母可能会决定寻求其他选择。菲尔兹-史密斯说：“我理解不想把你的孩子送到COVID背景下的学校。”但是，由于各种家庭都面临着潜在的挑战，每个人似乎都在为自己着想，没有明确的方法来帮助其他家庭茁壮成长。“如果你想一想美国文化，你会发现它有很多粗暴的个人主义，”她说。
家庭教育倾向于将社区大致分为基督徒和嬉皮士，而莎伦·杰克逊更多地属于后者而不是前者。她和她的丈夫都是私人教练和健身顾问-杰克逊曾在锐步全国有氧锦标赛上获得第二名，这项锦标赛涉及的五颜六色的弹力丝和你想象的一样多-而且她没有朝九晚五的时间表。当她阅读有关在家上学的资料时，她发现自己被“非学校教育”的概念所吸引，该概念声称，孩子们在指导自己的学习时学习得更好，而不是遵循既定的课程。
这是我一次又一次从在家上学倡导者那里听到的论点：非传统学校不仅仅是对正规学校的恐惧。它促进了学术上的卓越。“人们头脑中有这样一个想法，那就是什么是在家上学，但事实并非如此，”古典谈话公司的首席执行官罗伯特·博尔廷斯告诉我。“人们想到的是20世纪80年代，穿牛仔裙的在家上学的人，现在已经不是这样了。”他说，与2019年7月相比，他的公司今年7月的网站访问量翻了一番以上。罗布和珍·斯奈德(Rob And Jen Snyder)是一个基督教组织Leah的负责人，该组织自称是纽约最大的在家上学团体。他们告诉我，去年夏天该州废除了疫苗要求的宗教豁免后，人们对他们的兴趣激增。虽然家长们一直在问如何在家教育他们的孩子，以便他们能够继续传统的学校教育，但罗布·斯奈德说，“没有回到正常的道路上来。”他认为，这场流行病将永久性地重塑家长对学校的看法。
在各个方面，这场流行病都暴露了美国公共基础设施的弱点：医疗保健系统不能广泛地服务于每一个需要护理的人。感染率和死亡率的巨大差距，突显了美国现有的不平等。一个依赖于孩子们面对面在一起才能发挥作用的教育系统。许多美国最脆弱的家庭整个秋天都将在零星的远程学习设置中苦苦挣扎，收集儿童保育保险，并照顾那些在病例死灰复燃时不可避免地生病的家庭成员。对于那些已经倾向于怀疑这一体系的人来说，大流行刚刚证实了他们对美国的怀疑。杰克逊在一封电子邮件中写道：“自由是一种与生俱来的权利。”“主流媒体只是铺天盖地的宣传来控制人们，一个年轻人的思想是如此易受影响，这不是我想要抚养索菲亚的方式。”
不过，即使是在家上学，今年秋天也会有所不同。一年中的这个时候，数百个在家上学的家庭通常会聚集在中央公园的大山上。他们称之为“不回学校野餐”--这是一个与志同道合的家庭进行社交的机会，这些家庭在离开传统学校的过程中找到了类似的自由。这在今年不会发生，因为纽约的规定限制了大型集会。而在平时，许多在家上学的人实际上在家呆的时间很少，所以这些学生会感受到博物馆关闭和课外课程取消的影响。尽管如此，杰克逊告诉我，她对开始索菲亚的学习感到兴奋。她说，如果没有大流行，“我只会呆在这个系统里。”

looks out on the mid-pandemic landscape of New York City, she sees a scary place for a sixth grader. Her daughter, Sophia, just graduated from the kind of Lower Manhattan public school where the PTA can easily raise money for iPads and SMART Boards. Jackson liked that Sophia’s days were structured, and that she was able to make a handful of close friends. The past six months, however, have left Jackson wary of how school might affect her child. For one thing, Sophia doesn’t like wearing masks. “If we go to Whole Foods, she’ll put it on, but she just feels very restricted,” Jackson told me. At home, there’s no need for such barriers: “She can do her studies in her underwear.”
   Jackson has started noticing more unhoused people in the park near her apartment in Tribeca. The city “feels less safe in general to me right now,” Jackson, who is white, said, citing “the rioting, the rowdiness, the   random acts of violence happening.” (While shootings have spiked in New York this summer, the overall crime rate has remained flat, and far below the high crime levels of the 1980s and ’90s,  according to a recent  New York Times report.) With protesters calling on the government to defund the police, she feels as if “there’s less protection” in the city. With families facing economic hardship and the city on edge, Jackson fears Sophia could be exposed to danger. “You don’t know if there’s a kid in the classroom [whose] parents are going through a tough time, and maybe that child would act out and snap and decide they want to hit another kid,” she said. And when a vaccine for COVID-19 eventually arrives, Jackson is worried that New York officials will make proof of vaccination mandatory for kids to attend public schools. “I’m not comfortable with a vaccine that’s not rigorously tested,” Jackson said. “To expose her to something that just is so questionable doesn’t seem like a sound decision.” Before the pandemic, she never wanted to homeschool her daughter. Now, she said, it seems like her best option.
    COVID-19 has created a strange natural experiment in American education: Families who would have never otherwise considered taking their kids out of school feel desperate enough to try it. Reopening has been chaotic: In New York City, the start of school has been pushed back to late September, as teachers and principals   scramble to prepare for a semester split between online and in-person learning, fighting to secure the extra staffing and testing needed to safely bring kids back to class.
   Homeschooling organizations and consultants have faced a deluge of panicked parents frantic to find alternatives to regular school. Some families hate the idea of their kids sitting on Zoom for hours at a time. Others worry about exposing family members to the coronavirus or seeing schools close suddenly after a surge in cases. Although some of these parents will likely put their kids back in school once the pandemic is under control, homeschooling advocates see this period as an unlikely opportunity to evangelize their way of life, which they describe as more flexible, creative, and adaptable to each student than traditional school. Homeschooling families, which   included roughly 3 percent of school-age children in the United States in 2016, have lots of different reasons for wanting to educate their own kids. But they’re united in a common assessment: They want out of the traditional system. The question is whether COVID-19 will cause a temporary bump in homeschooling as parents piece together their days during the pandemic or mark a permanent inflection point in education that continues long after the virus has been controlled. Some families may find that they want to exit the system for good.
  , Sophia pushed her way through the end of the school year in the spring, graduating from elementary school in a quickly coordinated Zoom ceremony. But Jackson wasn’t satisfied with the thought of another cobbled-together semester, so over the summer, she started investigating alternative options for the fall. She found her way to Joanna Allen Lodin, one of many former homeschooling moms who have set up shop as small-time sages for families interested in leaving the traditional school system. Since May, Lodin has received a “snowball” of requests for information, she told me, and has hosted one or two information sessions a week for curious families.
 “I’m hearing everything, from parents who thought this spring was a disaster for their kids and who feel that they could do it better,” she said, to “people who said this spring was revelatory” and loved having their kids at home, concluding that “school is just babysitting, and I can do this better, and I’m going to homeschool them now.” The common thread, she said, is that “parents are terrified of failing their children.”
    A wide range of parents are attempting to homeschool this fall, and families with experience are trying to help them along. Kristen Rhodes, a former public-school special-education teacher who lives near the Georgia-Florida border, decided not to put her 5-year-old son in kindergarten this year, because she was worried about him having to wear a mask, and instead joined a group of fellow Christian parents and kids who use a curriculum called Classical Conversations. Nicole Damick, a homeschooling mom of four in Pennsylvania, has been eager to talk up homeschooling to curious friends and acquaintances: Life is lovelier with kids around, she wrote me in an email, “instead of forcing them off every morning with a crappy sandwich to endure the small daily abuses of a system that treats them like a value-added commodity to shoot out the other end of the K–12 pipeline.” Erik and Emily Orton, who homeschooled their five kids in New York City long before the pandemic, have been fielding questions from families worried about the cost to families who hope their nanny might become their kid’s educator, which the Ortons had never heard of before COVID-19. “The larger misperception is that it’s expensive, that it’s complicated, and that it’s time-consuming,” Erik Orton told me. “In our experience, it’s none of those things.”
   The pandemic may play into some of the instincts of parents inclined toward homeschooling. There’s “this notion that school itself is kind of a risky place for children: They’re too fragile, that they’re more likely to get sick,” Mitchell Stevens, an education professor at Stanford University, told me. “If you have school anxiety about your child, COVID is your worst nightmare, because school is not a civic community; it’s a public-health risk.” American history is filled with people making the civic case for common schooling. Horace Mann, the 19th-century education reformer, argued that public school is essential for forming prudential citizens. This idea has never fully won out in American culture, however. The homeschooling world is dominated by parents “who believe that their family comes first and are less concerned with public health or the public good,” Jennifer Lois, a professor at Western Washington University, told me. These parents often “end up choosing those kind of family-first” options.
  The problem is that in the chaos of the pandemic, it’s not clear how much common good any kind of school is doing. The children most likely to suffer under hybrid models of remote and in-person learning are those who don’t have access to the internet or whose parents have to work long hours outside the house, Cheryl Fields-Smith, an associate professor at the University of Georgia who studies Black homeschoolers, told me. These kids may have few other options—no matter how bad things get this fall, they’ll likely be stuck in traditional schools, while parents with more resources may decide to pursue alternatives. “I understand not wanting to send your child to school in a COVID context,” Fields-Smith said. But as families of all kinds face a potentially challenging fall, everyone seems to be in it for themselves, with no clear way to help other families thrive. “If you think about the American culture, it’s a lot of rugged individualism,” she said.
    of homeschooling tend to divide the community roughly into Christians and hippies, and Sharon Jackson falls more into the latter camp than the former. She and her husband both work as personal trainers and fitness consultants—Jackson once won second place at the Reebok National Aerobic Championship, which involves exactly as much colorful spandex as you’d   imagine—and she’s not on a 9-to-5 schedule. As she read up on homeschooling, she found herself attracted to the concept of “unschooling,” which claims that children learn better when they direct their own studies, rather than following a set curriculum.
  This was the argument I heard again and again from homeschooling advocates: Nontraditional schooling isn’t just about fear of regular school. It promotes academic excellence. “People have this idea in their head of what homeschooling is, and it isn’t,” Robert Bortins, the CEO of Classical Conversations, told me. “The thing people think about is the 1980s, jean-denim-skirt homeschoolers, and that’s not how it is anymore.” His company saw more than double the visitors to its website in July of this year compared with July 2019, he said. Rob and Jen Snyder, who oversee LEAH, a Christian organization that says it is the largest homeschooling group in New York, told me that they’ve gotten a huge surge in interest, building on last summer’s exodus from schools after the state  repealed a religious exemption to vaccine requirements. While parents have been asking how to homeschool their children so that they can stay on track with traditional school, “there is no going back to normal,” Rob Snyder said. He believes that the pandemic will permanently reshape how parents think about school.
   On every front, the pandemic has revealed the weaknesses in America’s public infrastructure: a health-care system that   cannot broadly serve everyone who needs care. Sharp disparities in infection and death rates that underscore America’s   existing inequalities. An education system that depends on children being together, in person, to function. Many of America’s most vulnerable families are going to spend the fall struggling through scattershot remote learning setups, assembling child-care coverage, and caring for family members who will inevitably get sick in a resurgence of cases. For people who were already inclined to doubt the system, the pandemic has just confirmed their suspicions about America. “Freedom is a state of being that is one’s birthright,” Jackson wrote me in an email. “The mainstream media just spews out propaganda to control people and a young person’s mind is so impressionable and that’s not how I want to raise Sophia.”
 Even homeschooling will look different this fall, though. Around this time of year, a few hundred homeschooling families typically gather on the Great Hill in Central Park. They call it the “not-back-to-school picnic”—a chance to socialize with like-minded families who have found similar freedom in leaving traditional school behind. That won’t be happening this year, because of New York’s rules limiting large gatherings. And in normal times, many homeschoolers actually spend little time at home, so these students will feel the effects of museum closures and extracurricular-class cancellations. Still, Jackson told me that she is excited to begin Sophia’s studies. Without the pandemic, she said, “I would have just been staying in the system.”

           is a staff writer at ​ The Atlantic, where she covers politics, policy, and religion.
title: 这场流行病导致家长逃离学校-也许是永远的
title_en: The Pandemic Has Parents Fleeing from Schools–Maybe Forever

note:
甲骨文显然赢得了接管TikTok美国业务的竞争。微软周日披露，TikTok的中国所有者ByteDance在当天早些时候拒绝了微软的竞争报价。
华尔街日报报道称，这笔交易不会直接出售。相反，甲骨文将成为字节跳动在美国的技术合作伙伴。这笔交易的细节尚未公开，但很快就会提交给美国监管机构审批。
这对拉里·埃里森(Larry Ellison)来说是一个胜利，他是甲骨文(Oracle)董事长，也是为数不多的公开支持唐纳德·特朗普(Donald Trump)的科技大亨之一。埃里森曾在2月份为特朗普举办了一场筹款活动。
8月，一名记者向特朗普询问甲骨文TikTok交易的可能性。特朗普的反应非常有利。
我认为甲骨文是一家伟大的公司，我认为它的所有者是一个了不起的人，特朗普说。他是个了不起的人。我认为甲骨文肯定是能处理这件事的人。
字节跳动面临的一大挑战是需要满足美国和中国政府潜在的相互冲突的要求。美国政府威胁要关闭TikTok，因为担心中国政府会损害美国人的隐私，或者对美国人看到的内容施加不适当的影响。将TikTok的美国业务转移给一家美国公司可能会消除这些担忧。
但中国政府对美国政府实质上为了美国竞争对手的利益而夺取中国一项重大科技资产的可能性感到不满。上月底，中国政府宣布了限制人工智能技术销售的新出口管制规定--这些规定显然适用于TikTok用来向其用户推荐视频的算法。这意味着ByteDance需要得到中国当局-以及特朗普政府的批准-才能通过任何交易。
有传言称，中国官员可能更愿意看到TikTok关闭，而不是开创外国政府夺取中国科技平台控制权的先例。字节跳动决定与甲骨文达成交易，而不是直接出售，可能是为了尊重中国政府的敏感性。
但安抚中国政府的努力很容易激怒美国监管机构在任何交易上的态度。如果特朗普政府拒绝ByteDance提出的交易，TikTok很有可能被关闭，ByteDance将一无所获。
因此，如果ByteDance认为拉里·埃里森(Larry Ellison)可以利用他与特朗普的私人关系来帮助交易获得批准，这将是选择甲骨文的一个令人信服的理由。
好的一面是，如果你不喜欢TikTok，甲骨文可能会在明年改变它的使用和许可条款，让它变得昂贵得令人望而却步，令人困惑。
note_en:
Oracle has apparently won the competition to take over TikTok's US operations. Microsoft  disclosed on Sunday that ByteDance, TikTok's Chinese owner, rejected Microsoft's rival bid earlier in the day.
 The Wall Street Journal  reports that the deal will not be an outright sale. Instead, Oracle will become ByteDance's US "tech partner." The details of the transaction aren't yet public but they will soon be submitted to US regulators for their approval.
  It's a victory for Larry Ellison, the chairman of Oracle and one of the few technology tycoons who has been openly supportive of Donald Trump. Ellison  held a fundraiser for Trump in February.
 In August, a reporter asked Trump about the possibility of an Oracle TikTok deal. Trump had a  favorable reaction.
 "I think Oracle is a great company, and I think its owner is a tremendous guy," Trump said. "He's a tremendous person. I think that Oracle would be certainly somebody that could handle it."
  The big challenge facing ByteDance is the need to to satisfy the potentially conflicting demands of the US and Chinese governments. The US government has threatened to shut down TikTok over concerns that the Chinese government would compromise Americans' privacy or exercise undue influence over the content Americans see. Transferring TikTok's US operations to an American company could address those concerns.
 But the Chinese government isn't happy about the possibility of the US government essentially seizing a major Chinese technology asset for the benefit of a US competitor. Late last month, Beijing  announced new export control rules restricting the sale of artificial intelligence technology—rules that apparently apply to the algorithm TikTok uses to recommend videos to its users. This means that ByteDance will need the approval of the Chinese authorities—as well as the Trump administration—before any deal can go through.
 Rumors have suggested that Chinese officials might prefer to see TikTok shut down than to create a precedent for foreign governments to seize control of Chinese technology platforms. ByteDance's decision to make the Oracle deal something less than an outright sale might be an effort to respect the sensitivities of the Chinese government.
 But efforts to mollify the Chinese government could easily sour US regulators on any deal. And if the Trump administration rejects ByteDance's proposed deal, there's a significant chance that TikTok will get shut down and ByteDance will get nothing.
 So if ByteDance believed Larry Ellison could use his personal relationship to Trump to get the deal approved, that would have been a compelling reason to choose Oracle.
  Well on the bright side if you don't like TikTok, Oracle will probably change the usage and licensing terms for it next year and make it prohibitively expensive and confusing.
title: 唐纳德·特朗普最喜欢的公司之一甲骨文赢得TikTok交易
title_en: Oracle, one of Donald Trump’s favorite companies, wins TikTok deal

note:
商业报道据一位未获授权公开谈论这笔交易的知情人士透露，TikTok的所有者已选择甲骨文(Oracle)而不是微软(Microsoft)作为收购这款广受欢迎的视频分享应用的首选追求者。
微软周日宣布，其收购TikTok的报价已被拒绝，并在唐纳德·特朗普(Donald Trump)总统承诺实施在美国禁止TikTok的计划的一周前，罢免了这款中国拥有的应用程序的主要追求者。
特朗普政府威胁要在9月1日之前禁止TikTok。20并命令ByteDance出售其美国业务，声称由于其中国所有权存在国家安全风险。政府担心用户数据会被泄露给中国当局。TikTok否认这是国家安全风险，并正在提起诉讼，以阻止政府受到威胁的禁令。
TikTok周日拒绝置评。甲骨文没有回复记者的置评请求，但此前拒绝置评。
微软在周日的一份声明中表示，TikTok的母公司字节跳动今天让我们知道，他们不会将TikTok的美国业务出售给微软。
沃尔玛曾计划在这笔交易上与微软合作。在微软周日接到拒绝通知之前，尚不清楚沃尔玛是否仍有兴趣。
微软周日表示，它相信我们的提议将有利于TikTok的用户，同时保护国家安全利益。该公司表示，它将做出重大改变，以确保该服务在安全、隐私、在线安全和打击虚假信息方面达到最高标准。
TikTok自称拥有1亿美国用户和全球约7亿用户，以其有趣、愚蠢的舞蹈、假唱、恶作剧和笑话视频而闻名。最近，它成为了更多政治内容的发源地，比如喜剧演员莎拉·库珀(Sarah Cooper)，她在公开露面时假唱特朗普自己经常脱节的声明，吸引了大量观众。
但由于其中国所有者ByteDance，这款应用也引发了担忧。白宫打击了一系列中国企业，包括电信设备制造商华为和中兴通讯以及即时通讯应用微信，原因是担心这些企业会让中国当局获得美国用户数据。共和党和民主党议员也提出了对审查制度和儿童隐私的担忧。
TikTok否认与中国政府共享用户数据，也否认如果要求它会这样做。该公司说，它没有应中国当局的要求审查视频，并坚称这不是对国家安全的威胁。
TikTok已经提起诉讼，要求停止禁令，但不是销售订单。销售形势因几个因素而变得复杂，包括特朗普一再要求美国政府在任何交易中都应获得减税，专家称这对总统的规定和作用是史无前例的。
此外，中国政府在8月下旬参与了这一过程，公布了限制技术出口的新规定，其中似乎包括TikTok用来选择将哪些视频上传给用户的系统。这意味着ByteDance必须从中国获得许可证，才能向外国公司出口任何受限制的技术。
在政府今年夏天加大了对TikTok的威胁之后，这笔交易迅速达成，尽管TikTok努力拉近其应用与其中国所有权之间的距离。该公司任命前迪士尼高管凯文·梅耶尔(Kevin Mayer)为其美籍首席执行官，但他在上任仅几个月后于8月份辞职，称政治环境已经发生了急剧变化。
微软和甲骨文都以其商业软件产品而闻名，而不是那些面向消费者的产品。
甲骨文主要生产数据库软件。它与提供云服务的微软和亚马逊等科技巨头以及Salesforce等商业软件专家展开竞争。
一些分析师认为甲骨文对消费者业务的兴趣是被误导的。杰富瑞(Jefferies)分析师布伦特·蒂尔(Brent Thill)说，甲骨文应该把重点放在企业市场收购上，而不是投资于TikTok这样与其其他业务不相适应的消费者应用。他将这一想法比作达美航空(Delta Airlines)收购了一家摩托车公司。这没有任何意义，他说。
蒂尔建议，像Facebook和Snapchat这样的TikTok竞争对手应该作为买家为甲骨文欢呼，因为甲骨文不会为这款应用增加很多价值。
甲骨文联合创始人拉里·埃里森(Larry Ellison)在科技高管中不同寻常，因为他公开支持唐纳德·特朗普(Donald Trump)总统。今年2月，埃里森在他位于加利福尼亚州兰乔米拉奇的庄园为他主持了一场筹款活动。该公司还聘请了副总统迈克·彭斯(Mike Pence)的一名前高级助手；其首席执行官萨夫拉·卡茨(Safra Catz)也曾在特朗普的过渡团队任职。
总统在8月18日表示，甲骨文是一家伟大的公司，可以处理收购TikTok的问题。他拒绝说明他更喜欢甲骨文和微软作为买家。
note_en:
Business      The owner of TikTok has chosen Oracle over Microsoft as its preferred suitor to buy the popular video-sharing app, according to a source familiar with the deal who was not authorized to speak publicly about it.
  Microsoft announced Sunday that its bid to buy TikTok has been rejected, removing a leading suitor for the Chinese-owned app a week before President Donald Trump promises to follow through with a plan to ban it in the U.S.
   The Trump administration has threatened to ban TikTok by Sept. 20 and ordered ByteDance to sell its U.S. business, claiming national-security risks due to its Chinese ownership. The government worries about user data being funneled to Chinese authorities. TikTok denies it is a national-security risk and is suing to stop the administration from the threatened ban.
  TikTok declined to comment Sunday. Oracle didn't return a request for comment but has previously declined comment.
  Microsoft said in a Sunday statement that TikTok's parent company, Bytedance, "let us know today they would not be selling TikTok's US operations to Microsoft."
    Walmart had planned to partner with Microsoft on the deal. It's not clear if Walmart was still interested before Microsoft was notified of the rejection Sunday.
  Microsoft said Sunday it was "confident our proposal would have been good for TikTok's users, while protecting national security interests." The company said it "would have made significant changes to ensure the service met the highest standards for security, privacy, online safety, and combating disinformation."
    TikTok, which says it has 100 million U.S. users and about 700 million globally, is known for its fun, goofy videos of dancing, lip-syncing, pranks and jokes. It's recently become home to more political content such as the comedian Sarah Cooper, who drew a large audience by lip-syncing Trump's own often-disjointed statements from public appearances.
  But the app has also raised concerns because of its Chinese owner, ByteDance. The White House has cracked down on a range of Chinese businesses, including telecom equipment makers Huawei and ZTE and messaging app WeChat, over worries that they would enable Chinese authorities to get U.S. user data. Republican and Democratic lawmakers have also raised concerns about censorship and children's privacy.
  TikTok denies that it has shared user data with the Chinese government or that it would do so if asked. The company says it has not censored videos at the request of Chinese authorities and insists it is not a national-security threat.
      TikTok has sued to stop the ban, but not the sale order. The sale situation has been complicated by several factors, including Trump's repeated demands that the U.S. government should get a "cut" of any deal, a stipulation and role for the president that experts say is unprecedented.
  In addition, the Chinese government in late August involved itself in the process by unveiling new regulations that restrict exports of technology that appear to include the system TikTok uses to choose which videos to spool up to its users. That means ByteDance would have to obtain a license from China to export any restricted technologies to a foreign company.
  The deal had come together rapidly after the administration ramped up its threats against TikTok this summer, despite TikTok's efforts to put distance between its app and its Chinese ownership. It installed former top Disney executive Kevin Mayer as its American CEO, but he resigned in August after just a few months on the job, saying the "political environment has sharply changed."
    Both Microsoft and Oracle are known more for their business software offerings than for those intended for consumers.
  Oracle primarily makes database software. It competes with tech giants such as Microsoft and Amazon that provide cloud services as well as business-software specialists like Salesforce.
  Some analysts see Oracle's interest in a consumer business as misguided. Oracle should focus on enterprise-market acquisitions and not invest in a consumer app like TikTok that doesn't fit with the rest of its business, said Jefferies analyst Brent Thill, who compares the idea to Delta Airlines buying a motorcycle company. "It doesn't make any sense," he said.
    Thill suggested that TikTok competitors like Facebook and Snapchat should be "cheering on Oracle" as a buyer, because Oracle wouldn't "add a lot of value to the app."
  Oracle co-founder Larry Ellison is unusual among tech executives for his public support of President Donald Trump, hosting a fundraiser for him in February at his Rancho Mirage, California, estate. The company also hired a former top aide to Vice-President Mike Pence; its CEO, Safra Catz, also served on Trump's transition team.
  The president said on Aug. 18 that Oracle was "a great company" that "could handle" buying TikTok. He declined to state his preference between Oracle and Microsoft as buyers.
title: TikTok在特朗普强制销售竞标中选择甲骨文而不是微软
title_en: TikTok Picks Oracle Over Microsoft In Trump-forced Sales Bid

note:
在当今的生物技术版图中，想要利用人工智能进行药物发现的初创公司比比皆是。但递归制药，这家历史最悠久、历史最悠久的人工智能药物发现公司之一，已经找到了脱颖而出的方法。周三上午，该公司宣布了2.39亿美元的D系列超额认购融资，并与制药巨头拜耳建立了利润丰厚的战略合作伙伴关系。
这笔资金包括拜耳投资部门Leaps来自拜耳的5,000万美元，以及来自Casdin Capital、Baillie Gifford和其他投资者的资金。Recursion还将从拜耳获得额外的3000万美元的预付款，用于开发治疗慢性瘢痕组织或纤维化的肺、肾和心脏疾病的新疗法。
“就数字药物发现而言，递归是我们的豪赌，”拜耳Leaps by负责人尤尔根·埃克哈特(Juergen Eckhardt)说。我们选择与Recursion合作，因为我们认为他们是最有机会尽快提供对患者有意义的药物的公司。“。
与典型的以使患病细胞健康为目标的药物发现不同，盐湖城的递归疗法从使健康细胞患病开始。该公司首席执行官兼联合创始人克里斯·吉布森(Chris Gibson)将其实验室描述为一个仓库，里面堆满了机器人和“非常非常小的培养皿”，每周进行100万次实验。这些实验包括以各种方式使人类细胞患病，拍摄这些患病细胞的照片，然后使用机器学习来了解患病细胞与健康细胞的不同之处。吉布森说，通过这种方式，他们能够找出哪些药物能将患病细胞恢复为健康细胞。
“这有点颠覆了药物发现的前几步，”吉布森说。“我们喜欢让生物学给我们答案。”
许多其他人工智能药物发现公司通过梳理现有的大型数据集来发现新药，而不是通过实验来开发新的数据。Recursion成立于2013年，聘请科学家和软件工程师将他们的整个流水线保持在内部，从创建海量数据集到在临床开发过程中服用药物。吉布森说：“我们把重点放在建立全套药物发现和开发上。”
Reversion目前有四种药物在人体试验中，包括治疗罕见疾病神经纤维瘤病2型的药物，这种疾病涉及神经系统中生长的非癌肿瘤，以及脑海绵状畸形，这是大脑中的异常血管。大脑海绵状畸形，也称为CCM，影响0.5%的人口，而2型神经纤维瘤病在全球每33000人中就有1人受到影响。这些疾病可能是遗传性的，可能会导致慢性残疾甚至死亡。虽然该公司计划保持对罕见疾病治疗的开发的控制，但它正在更具竞争力的领域与更大的制药公司合作。
与拜耳的新合作伙伴关系将专门专注于纤维化疾病，这种疾病可能会导致瘢痕组织干扰器官功能。这项合作涉及一项开始开发至少十个新治疗项目的协议，每个项目将获得1亿美元的里程碑式付款，以及未来销售的特许权使用费。两家公司将共同拥有这些项目，拜耳将有权独家许可候选药物。拜耳并不是唯一一家与Recursion合作的大型制药公司：近年来，该公司还与武田(Takeda)和赛诺菲(Sanofi)签署了数百万美元的药物研发协议。
吉布森说，新的D系列资本将用于扩大公司规模，继续药物开发。他预计会有大约50个新的工作岗位出现，并表示该公司的四个顶级候选药物将在2021年进入第二阶段关键试验。在最近于2020年7月收购数字隔膜公司Vium之后，该公司可能还在寻找更多的收购对象。吉布森说：“我们并不抱有幻想，认为只有我们创造了奇妙的技术来颠覆这个空间。”“我们有兴趣建立更多的合作伙伴关系，在某些领域已经朝着一些方向发展。”
note_en:
Startups that want to leverage artificial intelligence in drug discovery are a dime a dozen in today’s biotech landscape. But Recursion Pharmaceuticals, one of the oldest and most established AI drug discovery companies, has found a way to stand out. The company announced on Wednesday morning an oversubscribed series D funding round of $239 million, and a lucrative strategic partnership with pharma giant Bayer.
  The funding includes $50 million from Leaps by Bayer, Bayer’s investment arm, as well as funding from Casdin Capital, Baillie Gifford and other investors. Recursion will also receive an additional $30 million upfront payment from Bayer to develop new treatments for chronic scar tissue, or fibrotic, diseases of the lung, kidneys and heart.
  “Recursion is our big bet in terms of digital drug discovery,” says Juergen Eckhardt, head of Leaps by Bayer. “We chose to partner with Recursion as we saw them as the company with the best chance to deliver medicines that could be meaningful to patients as quickly as possible.”
    Unlike typical drug discovery, which has the goal of making sick cells healthy, the Salt Lake City-based Recursion starts by making healthy cells sick. Chris Gibson, the company’s CEO and cofounder, describes its lab as a warehouse filled with robots and “really, really tiny petri dishes” that perform one million experiments a week. The experiments involve making human cells sick in a variety of ways, taking pictures of these sick cells, and then using machine learning to understand how the sick cells differ from healthy cells. In this way, Gibson says, they are able to figure out which drugs turn the sick cell back into a healthy cell.
  “It’s kind of flipping the first few steps of drug discovery,” Gibson says. “We like to let biology give us the answer.”
  Many other AI drug discovery companies find new drugs by combing through large, existing datasets, rather than experimenting themselves to develop new data. Recursion, which was founded in 2013, employs both scientists and software engineers to keep their entire pipeline in-house, from creating massive datasets to taking their drugs through clinical development. “We have a focus on building the full stack of drug discovery and development,” Gibson says.
    Recursion currently has four drugs in human testing, including medications for rare diseases  neurofibromatosis type 2, a disease that involves noncancerous tumors growing in the nervous system, and  cerebral cavernous malformations, which are abnormal blood vessels in the brain. Cerebral cavernous malformations, also called CCMs, affect 0.5% of the population, while neurofibromatosis type 2 affects 1 in 33,000 people worldwide. These diseases, which can be genetic, may cause chronic disability and even death. While the company plans to maintain control of development for rare disease treatments, it is partnering with larger pharmaceutical companies in more competitive areas.
  The new partnership with Bayer will specifically focus on fibrotic diseases, which can cause scar tissue that interferes with organ function. The partnership involves an agreement to begin the development of at least ten new treatment programs, with milestone payments of $100 million per program and royalties on future sales. Both companies will jointly own the projects, and Bayer will have the option to exclusively license the drug candidates. Bayer isn’t the only big pharma company that Recursion has partnered with: in recent years the company has also inked multi-million dollar drug discovery deals with  Takeda and  Sanofi.
  Gibson says the new series D capital will be used to expand the company and continue its drug development. He anticipates about 50 new jobs opening up and says that the company’s four top drug candidates will go into phase 2 pivotal trials in 2021. The company may also be on the lookout for more acquisitions following the recent July 2020 purchase of digital vivarium company  Vium. “We’re under no illusions that we’re the only ones creating fantastic technologies to disrupt the space,” Gibson says. “We’re interested in building additional partnerships, and in some areas are already heading towards some.”
title: 人工智能药物发现公司Recursion筹集2.39亿美元D系列资金，并与制药巨头拜耳合作开发纤维化疾病的新疗法
title_en: AI-based drug discovery company Recursion raises $239M Series D and partners with pharma giant Bayer to develop new treatments for fibrotic diseases

note:
罗尔德·阿蒙森(Roald Amundsen)在罗伯特·斯科特(Robert Scott)之前登上南极，人们经常将阿蒙森描述为比赛的获胜者。但100多年过去了，关于这些事件，两人准备得如何，他们如何行事，运气扮演了什么角色，以及他们的遗产，仍存在争议。他们两人都带领五支男子队伍登上了极点，尽管阿蒙森的队伍活着回来了，但斯科特的队伍在返程中都死了。
众所周知的南极竞赛是偶然发生的，而不是故意的。阿蒙森和斯科特从未承认或计划过比赛，他们都计划进行探险，雄心勃勃地想成为那个时代最后一个伟大的地理目标之一-南极的第一个人。
几乎从一开始就存在争议，斯科特开始认为他的政党将是当时南极洲唯一有到达南极雄心的政党。阿蒙森本来要尝试北极，但当他听说美国人库克和佩里声称已经到达北极时，他改变了计划，担心他会在财务上破产(在他们向南行驶之前，甚至不会告诉他自己的船员)，因此单方面为一场比赛做好了准备。阿蒙森凯旋而归，在描述他的成就的巡回演讲中受到了好评，几个月后，当斯科特和他的团队也到达南极的消息传来时，情况变得糟糕起来，但在回来的路上都死了。颇具讽刺意味的是，斯科特和他的政党成为了这篇文章的英雄，斯科特本人在英国被认为是主要的民族英雄，而阿蒙森实际上并不被视为恶棍，他被忽略了，以获得进一步的赞誉。
斯科特被誉为最高尚的民族英雄几乎毫无疑问地持续了半个多世纪，直到20世纪末他的缺点开始被指出，他的故事开始招致批评。
阿蒙森在回来后发现生活很艰难，他被指控行为不端，在没有事先通知任何意图的情况下抵达南极洲与斯科特比赛到极点，这些指控更加痛苦，因为他和他的团队幸存下来，而斯科特和他的团队都灭亡了。他于1928年去世，当时被认为是飞机残骸后在北极坠毁，但没有发现身体。
阿蒙森和斯科特从成功和失败中学到了许多关于南极探险的经验，这对未来的探险将是无价的。他们的故事引人入胜的原因有很多，并将他们揭示为复杂的人物，受到一系列因素的影响，他们被世界和他们的国家接受的方式几乎同样复杂。我觉得对他们两人最大的赞美是，现在在南极有一个全年有人驾驶的大型科学站，叫做阿蒙森-斯科特南极站。
阿蒙森一行在南极，1911年12月14日，星期五，下午3点左右。
1912年1月18日，斯科特及其一行在挪威帐篷旁的南极
阿蒙森和斯科特在到达南极和之后的准备工作上的比较。谁赢了就很清楚了，尽管只有当你考虑到这是一场竞争的时候。阿蒙森的故事是斯科特使用狗拉雪橇和滑雪板在北极的经历。21岁时，母亲去世后，他放弃学业成为一名医生，转而从事极地探险的生活。
南极尝试的背景：弗里乔夫·南森(Fritjof Nansen)给了阿蒙森一艘弗拉姆号(Fram)，试图漂流到北极，他通过向他人寻求财政支持来支付补给和工资。1909年9月，美国人罗伯特·皮里(Robert Peary)声称已经到达北极，但阿蒙森的计划被毁了，如果他不能取得成功，他个人的财务可能也会因此破产。
他改变了计划，转而前往南极，没有告诉他的支持者，也没有公开，直到他很好地进行了，以防项目在开始之前就结束了。“弗拉姆”号的船长事先被告知，另外两名男子在出发当天被告知，其余船员直到“弗拉姆”号向南航行到据说是为了进行海洋学研究的马德拉时才被告知。阿蒙森声称，他认为斯科特的探险是科学的，只是北极是一个次要问题，尽管斯科特在近一年前公开宣布了一项关于尝试南极的计划。
路线：从鲸鱼湾出发，比斯科特离极点更近60英里，没有科学计划，不需要妥协。更多的旅行距离是在罗斯冰架上进行的，这意味着旅行中在极地高原海拔的部分较少，男性的活动效率会更高，工作时间会更长，承受更低的高海拔温度的时间也会更短。进入极地高原是通过登上阿蒙森发现的冰川来实现的，该冰川以他的一位赞助人的名字命名为阿克塞尔·海伯格冰川(Axel Heiberg Glacier)。阿蒙森和他的团队选择不走已知的比尔德莫尔冰川路线，因为那是斯科特和34；的边界。登上高原的路线有很多高度的增减(19590英尺对斯科特11470英尺)据说带着狗队更容易完成这段旅程，这在比尔德莫尔上会有更大的困难，尽管随后的评估发现这条路线本身非常困难，只有非常熟练和有经验的训练员团队才能尝试。
1901年至1904年，担任南极探索探险队队长3年。在探险队的50人中，几乎没有人有过任何极地经验，之前或正在进行的使用狗拉雪橇或滑雪等设备或技术的培训很少。
英国海军的一名军官在“发现号之旅”中写道：“我不妨承认，我对极地探险没有兴趣，这部分是由于家庭环境的减少。”
南极尝试的背景：斯科特在发现号探险之后以英雄的身份返回英国，他重新开始了他的海军生涯，现在被提升为船长。在发现号上，他与1906年开始计划自己的南极探险的欧内斯特·沙克尔顿闹翻了。斯科特觉得沙克尔顿没有权利去麦克默多地区，因为他之前有权去这个地区。
斯科特在很大程度上是权势人物，并被选为英国官方南极探险队的领队。斯科特表示，主要目标是到达南极，并为大英帝国确保这一成就的荣誉。斯科特安排购买小马、狗和机动雪橇，尽管他仍然喜欢拖车，他在海军的支持者也是如此。不管喜好如何，人们认为机动雪橇、狗和小马将无法登上高度破裂的比尔德莫尔冰川(Beardmore Glacier)，这条路线由沙克尔顿于1909年首创。虽然可以使用其他方法铺设仓库，但他们必须人工拖运到极点。
成为第一个到达南极的政党，这是斯科特所说的主要目标。
一个大而广泛的科学测量、实验和发现计划。科学补充=12。
路线：斯科特使他在罗斯岛埃文斯角的基地更适合进行科学探索，尽管距离极点较远，并受到海冰融化或形成的影响，决定何时可以尝试旅行的开始/结束。他要走的路线是沙克尔顿在他1907-1909年探险期间开辟的路线，沿着活跃的比尔德莫尔冰川到达极地高原，这条冰川非常破碎和破裂，减缓了进展。与阿蒙森通过又短又陡的阿克塞尔·海伯格冰川进入高原不同，这是一次漫长而缓慢的攀登，对狗、小马和摩托车雪橇来说应该更容易，尽管实际上情况更糟，因为它的性质被裂缝和冰川打破了。斯科特的旅程更长，他在空气稀薄、温度更低的极地高原上呆的时间更长。
交通工具：Amundsen在横跨西北航道的Gjoa旅行中与雪橇狗打交道有很多经验，他和因纽特人一起生活，并从他们那里学到了很多关于如何使用雪橇狗以及它们的优点和缺点。当时的挪威人倾向于将驯鹿作为群体动物。
他对这些狗的计划是，在旅行的早期用它们中的许多来负重，然后登上极地高原，然后杀死它们，在旅行的后期用它们来喂人和其他狗。一旦登上极地高原，52只被捕获的狗中有24只被杀，11只活着回来。所有的挪威人滑雪都滑得很好，奥拉夫·比亚兰凭借他作为滑雪冠军的优势被招募到探险队中。狗和滑雪者的速度意味着更长的休息时间是可能的。
天气：有证据表明，阿蒙森比斯科特更早离开和返回，避免了斯科特和他的团队恰好遇到的那一年异常恶劣的天气。
食物和燃料：阿蒙森的团队实际上在前往极地的旅途中体重增加了，部分原因是沿途被杀的狗提供了额外的食物。
阿蒙森在仓库铺设准备期间特别挑剔地标记路线，这意味着他的团队即使在能见度较低的条件下也可以旅行，他们有更频繁的食品库，阿蒙森仓库的食物数量是斯科特的三倍。用于炉灶的石蜡燃料在寒冷的温度下蠕动，也就是说，它从螺丝顶部泄漏到罐头并蒸发，阿蒙森确保所有的燃料罐也都焊接闭合，直到它们被打开，不会造成燃料损失。阿蒙森的雪橇配给包括燕麦片和培米根加豌豆，这会给他们提供有助于防止坏血病的维生素。
服装：挪威人穿着完全由毛皮制成的因纽特风格的衣服，这也是阿蒙森从他的Gjoa探险中学到的另一件事。
运动服穿得很松，这样可以让汗水在运动过程中流失，这意味着在休息时，汗水不会在衣服里结冰。
交通工具：斯科特的交通选择备受批评。虽然他带着机动雪橇、小马、狗和滑雪专家Tryggve Gran教他的人滑雪，但每一样东西都没有得到很好的利用。三个机动雪橇中最大的一个在被带上岸时丢失了，其余两个不可靠，使用专家斯凯尔顿在南极之旅中由于海军军衔协议的原因被留在了后面，其余两个雪橇失败了，无法修理。小马有雪鞋来防止它们在松软的雪中下沉，但它们没有被那么严格地使用，限制了它们的用途。这些人中没有足够多的人对狗足够熟练，以至于无法发挥作用，而且一开始就没有足够的狗。最后，虽然斯科特鼓励他的手下学习滑雪，但他们并不情愿，他也没有坚持要他们学习。他只能用最累人、最慢的拖运方法，尽管他认为这样做更高尚。
天气：天气记录显示，斯科特返程的天气特别糟糕，是十五年来的最低气温，当时阿蒙森已经回来了。
食物和燃料：理论测量和实际的现代实验表明，斯科特的团队没有足够的食物来满足他们的需要，当他们到达极点时，他们都处于相当糟糕的状态。斯科特已经计算过了，但他没有意识到在极地高原高度人工拖船时对食物的更大需求，所以低估了需要多少食物。斯科特忽略了石蜡罐中燃料蠕变损失的现象，尽管这是众所周知的，因此他的团队缺乏燃料可能也会导致脱水，因为更少的雪可以融化成水来弥补在低温运动中看似巨大的损失。几乎可以肯定的是，由于他们的雪橇口粮中缺乏维生素，他们也受到了坏血病的虚弱影响。当小马象阿蒙森的狗一样活得不够用时，它们就被杀了当食物。
着装：斯科特的男士们穿着外层防风的羊毛内衣，分层系统很好，现在也在使用，但在运动时允许足够的通风是一个问题，这意味着汗水冻结在衣服里，使他们变得坚硬，特别是在露营之后。
第一次尝试，1911年9月8日：阿蒙森担心斯科特的机动雪橇会让他第一个到达极点，并希望在8月下旬太阳再次升起后尽快出发。约翰森不顾天气太冷的建议，于1911年9月8日带着9人中的8人出发了。白天的旅行很好，尽管天气很冷，他们在最初的几天还是取得了很好的进展，但是晚上太冷了，他们几乎睡不着觉，一些狗还受到了冻伤的爪子的折磨。到了9月12日，气温达到零下56摄氏度，他们停了下来，建造了冰屋作为避难所，然后回到船上，稍后再试一次。一些狗在回来的路上冻死了，另一些太虚弱以至于跑不动的狗被放在雪橇上，阿蒙森命令他的人尽快赶回家，而不是结伴旅行。Amundsen和另外两个人在9个小时后第一个到达，两个小时后又坐了一次雪橇，最后两个人包括Johansen在出发后超过17个小时。约翰森愤怒地回答了阿蒙森关于他们为什么迟到这么晚的问题，因为他们觉得自己被遗弃了，没有食物和燃料，还遭受着冻伤。阿蒙森后来报告说，他觉得约翰森非常从属，因此把他排除在南极派对之外。
第二次尝试，1911年10月19日：阿蒙森带着五个人，四辆雪橇和五十二条狗再次出发。他们又一次取得了良好的进展，尽管阿蒙森刻意避开了斯科特从近海平面的冰架到南极高原所要走的比尔德莫尔冰川路线，但他找到了一条陡峭而困难但直接登上SMA的路线
他们停留在柱子附近，然后于18日启程返回。在此期间，他们在一天中的不同时间获取导航读数，并在附近旅行，以确保他们真的到达了南极。阿蒙森留下了一顶帐篷和一封写给挪威国王的信，要求斯科特把它交给他。他们于1912年1月25日回到弗拉姆，也就是在离开南极仅仅38天之后。整个旅程花了99天，比预期少了10天，他们已经走了3440公里(1860海里)，5个人和52条狗出发了，5个人和11条狗回来了。
1911年11月1日，斯科特带着乘坐机动雪橇、狗、小马和徒步旅行的人离开了他的营地。只有四个人的小队会一路走到极点，其他人在那里帮助运输外出旅行中使用的食物和补给，然后留在补给堆场，供极点小组在回来的路上使用。小马在这次游行中被杀，一些肉被吃掉，一些肉被运往仓库，就像阿蒙森对他的狗所做的那样。最终有两个队，每队四个人拖雪橇和粮食，尽管他原计划四个人，斯科特决定带一个五人小组到杆子上，包括他自己，剩下的三个人回到船上。斯科特一行在出发77天后，于1912年1月17日抵达北极。他们发现，阿蒙森比他们早了五周，此时由于饮食数量和营养不足，他们的病情正在恶化，坏血病可能开始起作用了。他们休息了一天，19日出发返回。尽管天气异常寒冷，他们的饮食和士气低落，但他们在极地高原的边缘取得了良好的进展。在比尔德莫尔冰川从高原下降到接近海平面一级的100英里的过程中，埃德加·埃文斯在2月4日摔倒，使他变得迟钝和无力，但他再也没有真正恢复过来，并于2月17日再次跌倒在冰川脚下附近死亡。斯科特在1912年3月1日8点30分左右给犬队下了命令，让他们与极地党会合，剩下的四个人到了2月27日就到了这一步。到3月10日，他们放弃了看到狗到达的机会，在艰难的地面条件和异常寒冷的温度下，他们还有大约400英里的路程要走。这一天，劳伦斯·奥茨船长脚被严重冻伤，在暴风雪中走出帐篷等死，他最后说的话是：我正要出去，可能过段时间再走。他们又走了20英里，现在都很痛苦，最后在3月19日安营扎寨。暴风雪阻碍了进一步的进展，他们呆在帐篷里，供应不断减少，斯科特的最后一篇日记是在3月29日，
.
note_en:
The attainment of the South Pole by Roald Amundsen ahead 					of Robert Scott has frequently described Amundsen as the winner 					in a race.  Over a hundred years later there is still debate 					about the events, how well the two men were prepared, how they 					conducted themselves, what role luck had to play and not least 					of their legacies. They both led five man teams to the pole, 					though while Amundsen's team returned alive and well, Scott's 					party all died on the return journey.
          What has become known as the Race to the South Pole 					came about incidentally rather than by design. At no 					time did Amundsen and Scott acknowledge or plan for a race, 					they both planned expeditions that had as an ambition to be 					the first man to reach one of the last great geographic goals 					of the age, the South Pole.
  There was controversy almost from the outset, 					Scott set off thinking his would be the only party in Antarctica 					with an ambition of reaching the South Pole at that time. Amundsen 					was to try for the North Pole but changed his plans when he 					heard that Americans Cook and Peary had claimed to have reached 					there, fearing he would be financially ruined (not even telling 					his own crew until they were headed south) and so set the scene 					unilaterally for a "race". Amundsen returned home in triumph 					and was well received on lecture tours describing his achievement, 					this was soured when months later the news arrived that Scott 					and his party had also reached the South Pole but had all perished 					on the way back. Somewhat ironically Scott and his party became 					the heroes of the piece, Scott himself being held as a major 					national hero in Britain, while Amundsen not actually seen as 					the villain, was passed over for further acclaim.
  The hailing of Scott as the most noble kind of national 					hero continued almost without question for more than half a 					century until his failings began to be pointed out 					and his story began to attract criticism towards the end of 					the 20th century.
  Amundsen found life difficult on his return, 					he was wounded by accusations of unsporting conduct in the manner 					that he arrived in Antarctica to "race" Scott to the pole without 					giving any prior notice of intention, these accusations were 					all the more painful because he and his team survived while 					Scott and his party all perished. He died in 1928 in what was 					assumed to be an airplane crash in the Arctic after wreckage 					but no bodies were found.
  Many lessons were learned about exploration in the Antarctic 					from both Amundsen and Scott from successes and failures that 					would be invaluable for future expeditions. Their stories are 					fascinating for many reasons and reveal them as complex characters 					affected by a whole host of factors, the way they were received 					by the world and their countries is almost as complex. I feel 					the greatest compliment to both of them is that there is now 					a large scientific station at the South Pole manned year round 					called the Amundsen-Scott South Pole Station.
    Amundsen and his party at the 							South Pole, 14th December 1911, Friday, about 							3 p.m.
  Scott and his party at the South 							Pole by the Norwegians tent, 18th January, 1912
   A comparison of Amundsen and Scott in relation to 					preparations to reach the South Pole and afterwards. Who "won" 					is clear, though only if you consider there was a competition.  Amundsen's story  					 Scott's story  					     Much experience in the Arctic 								using dog sleds and skis. At the age of 21 he abandoned 								his studies to be a doctor when his mother died 								and instead pursued a life of polar exploration.
  Background 							to the South Pole attempt: Amundsen 							was given the ship  Fram by Fritjof Nansen to 							attempt to drift across the North Pole, he paid for 							supplies and wages by asking others for financial backing. 							In September 1909, American Robert Peary claimed to 							have reached the North Pole, Amundsen's plans were ruined 							and he may have been personally financially ruined as 							a result if he couldn't deliver a success.
  He changed his plans and headed to the South Pole 							instead without telling his backers or making it public 							until he was well under way in case the project was 							ended before it begun. The captain of the  Fram 							was told in advance and two other men were told on the 							day of departure, the rest of the crew were not told 							until the  Fram had sailed south to Madeira 							supposedly for oceanographical research. Amundsen claimed 							that he thought Scott's expedition was scientific only 							with the Pole being a side issue, despite Scott making 							a public announcement nearly a year earlier about an 							attempt on the Pole.
      The route:  Started at the Bay of Whales, 60 miles 							closer to the pole than Scott, with no scientific programme 							no compromise needed to be made. More of the journey 							distance was made over the Ross Ice Shelf meaning that 							less of the journey was at the altitude of the Polar 							Plateau, the men would function more efficiently for 							longer and be subjected to colder high altitude temperatures 							for a shorter time. Access to the Polar Plateau was 							made by going up a glacier discovered by Amundsen and 							called the Axel Heiberg Glacier after one of his patrons. 							Amundsen and his team had chosen not to go the known 							Beardmore glacier route as that was Scott's and "out 							of bounds". The route up to the plateau involved much 							gain and loss of height (19,590 feet v Scott's 11,470 							feet) it has been said that it is easier to make this 							journey with a dog team which would have much greater 							difficulty on the Beardmore, though subsequent assessment 							has found this route is very difficult in itself, only 							a very skilled and experienced team of handlers could 							attempt it.
  3 years as leader of the Discovery Expedition 								to Antarctica, 1901-1904. Of 50 men on 								the expedition hardly any had any polar experience, 								there was very little prior or ongoing training 								to use equipment or techniques such as dog sledding 								or skiing.
  An officer in the British Navy seeking 								advancement partly due to reduced family circumstances, 								in "The Voyage of the Discovery" he wrote: "I may 								as well confess that I had no predilection for polar 								exploration".
  Background to 							the South Pole attempt: Scott returned 							to Britain a hero after the Discovery expedition, he 							resumed his naval career now promoted to captain. On 							the Discovery he had fallen out with Ernest Shackleton 							who was by 1906 planning his own Antarctic expedition. 							Scott felt Shackleton had no claim to go to the McMurdo 							area as he had prior "right" to this sector.
  Scott was very much the establishment man and was 							chosen to head the official British Antarctic Expedition, 							Scott stated that the main objective was  "to 							reach the South Pole, and to secure for the British 							Empire the honour of this achievement". Scott 							arranged for ponies, dogs and motor sleds to be purchased 							though still favoured manhauling as did his backers 							in the navy. Regardless of preference, it was thought 							that motor sleds, dogs and ponies would not be able 							to ascend the highly crevassed Beardmore Glacier, the 							route pioneered by Shackleton in 1909. While other means 							could be used to lay depots, they would have to manhaul 							to the pole.
    To be the first party to reach the South 								Pole, stated by Scott as being the main 								aim.
  A large and wide ranging programme of 								scientific measurements, experimentation and discovery.  Scientific complement = 12
  The route:  Scott made his base at Cape Evans on 							Ross Island a more suitable base for scientific explorations 							though further from the pole and subjected to sea ice 							melting or forming dictating when the start/end of the 							journey could be attempted. The route he was to take 							was that pioneered by Shackleton during his 1907-1909 							expedition, reaching the Polar Plateau along the active 							Beardmore Glacier which was very broken up and crevassed, 							slowing progress. Unlike Amundsen's access to the Plateau 							via the short and steep Axel Heiberg Glacier this was 							a long slow climb that should have been easier for dogs, 							ponies and motor sleds, though was actually worse due 							to its broken up nature with crevasses and ice-falls. 							Scott's journey was longer and he spent more time at 							the altitude of the Polar Plateau with its thinner air 							and colder temperatures.
            Means of Transport:   Amundsen had much experience 							with sled dogs during his trip in the Gjoa across the 							Northwest Passage, he had lived with and learned 							much from the Inuit about how to use them and what were 							their strengths and weaknesses. Norwegians at the time 							tended to use reindeer as pack animals.
  His plan with the dogs was to use many of them in 							the early part of trip with heavy loads and to get up 							onto the Polar Plateau, to then kill them and use them 							to feed the men and other dogs in the later part of 							the journey. Once up on the Polar Plateau, 24 dogs were 							killed out of 52 taken, 11 returned alive. All of the 							Norwegians could ski well, Olav Bjaaland was recruited 							to the expedition on his strengths as a champion skier. 							The speed of the dogs and skiers meant that longer periods 							of rest were possible.
  Weather:  There is evidence that Amundsen in leaving 							and returning earlier than Scott avoided unusually severe 							weather that year that Scott and his team walked right 							into.
  Food and Fuel:  Amundsen's team actually gained weight 							during the journey to the pole, partly due to the extra 							food available from the dogs that were killed along 							the route.
  Amundsen was particularly fastidious in marking the 							route during the depot laying preparations meaning that 							his team could travel even in poor visibility conditions, 							they had more frequent food depots, Amundsen depoted 							three times the amount of food that Scott did. The paraffin 							fuel used for the stoves "creeps" in cold temperatures, 							that is, it leaks out of the screw top to the can and 							evaporates, Amundsen made sure all fuel cans were also 							soldered closed until they were to be opened leading 							to no fuel losses. The sledging rations Amundsen took 							included oatmeal and pemmican with peas in, this would 							have given them vitamins that would have helped prevent 							scurvy.
  Clothing:  The Norwegians took Inuit style clothing 							made entirely out of furs, another thing Amundsen learned 							from his Gjoa expedition.
  They were worn loose to allow for the loss of sweat 							during exertion meaning that it didn't then freeze inside 							the clothing when at rest.
  Means of Transport:  Scott has been much criticized for his 							transport choices. While he took motor sleds, ponies, 							dogs and an expert skier Tryggve Gran, to teach his 							men to ski, each of these was poorly used. The largest 							of the three motor sleds was lost while being taken 							ashore, the remaining two were unreliable and Skelton 							the expert in their use, was left behind for reasons 							of naval rank protocols in the South Pole journey, the 							remaining two sleds failed and could not be fixed. The 							ponies had snow shoes to stop them sinking in soft snow 							but they were not used so severely limiting their usefulness. 							Not enough of the men were expert enough with the dogs 							to be effective and there were insufficient dogs in 							the first place. Finally while Scott encouraged his 							men to learn to ski, they were reluctant and he did 							not insist on them learning. He was left with the most 							exhausting and slowest method of manhauling, though 							he considered this to be more "noble".
  Weather:  Weather records show that the weather 							on the return journey for Scott was particularly bad, 							being a one in fifteen year temperature lows, by that 							time Amundsen had returned.
  Food and Fuel:  Theoretical measurements and practical 							modern experimentation has shown that Scott's team had 							insufficient food for their needs, they were all in 							quite a poor state by the time they reached the Pole. 							Calculations had been made by Scott, but he was not 							aware of the greater need for food while manhauling 							at the altitude of the Polar Plateau and so underestimated 							how much was needed. Scott ignored the phenomenon of 							"fuel creep" loss from the paraffin cans even though 							it was well known, consequently his team were short 							of fuel possibly also leading to dehydration as less 							snow could be melted for water to replace the deceptively 							large amount lost when exercising at low temperatures. 							They were almost certainly also suffering from the debilitating 							effects of scurvy too due to the lack of vitamins in 							their sledging rations. The ponies were killed for food 							when they had outlived their usefulness, like Amundsen's 							dogs.
  Clothing:  Scott's men had woolen underwear with 							windproof outer layers, the layer system was good and 							is used today, there was a problem with allowing for 							sufficient ventilation during exertion meaning that 							sweat froze in the clothes making them hard and stiff, 							especially after a camp.
            First attempt, 							8th September 1911: Amundsen was concerned 							by the idea that Scott's motor sledges would allow him 							to reach the pole first and wanted to set off as soon 							as possible after the sun rose again in late August.							Against the advice of Johansen that it was 							too cold, he set off on the 8th of September 1911 with 							eight of the nine men on his expedition. Day time travel 							was good and they made very good progress in the first 							few days despite very cold temperatures, it was so cold 							at night however that they could hardly sleep and some 							of the dogs suffered from frostbitten paws. By the 12th 							of September with temperatures of -56C they stopped 							and built igloos for shelter before returning to the 							ship to try again later. Some of the dogs froze to death 							on the way back, others too weak to run were placed 							on the sledges, Amundsen ordered his men to push for 							home as soon as possible rather than travelling as a 							group. Amundsen and two others arrived back first after 							nine hours, another sledge two hours later and the final 							two including Johansen over seventeen hours after setting 							off. Johansen answered Amundsen's question about why 							they had been so late angrily, feeling they had been 							abandoned without food or fuel and suffering from frostbite. 							Amundsen later reported that he felt Johansen was "violently 							subordinate" and excluded him from the South Pole party.
  Second attempt, 							19th October 1911:  Amundsen 							set out again with five men, four sledges and fifty 							two dogs. Once again they made good progress, 							though Amundsen deliberately avoided the Beardmore Glacier 							route that Scott would take to get from the near sea-level 							ice shelf and onto the Antarctic Plateau, he found a 							steep and difficult but direct route up a small glacier 							he named after Axel Heiberg, one of his financial backers. 							seven of his dogs had died on the ice barrier to reach 							the glacier, all but a remaining eighteen were killed 							at the top of the glacier, the carcasses were eaten 							eagerly by the dogs and men alike, some were depoted 							to be picked up on the return journey.  They 							reached the pole on the 14th of December 1911, 56 days 							after setting off.
  They stayed in the vicinity of the pole before setting 							off on their return journey on the 18th. During this 							time, they took navigational readings at different times 							of the day and travelled in the vicinity to make sure 							they really had reached the South Pole. Amundsen left 							a tent and a letter addressed to the King of Norway 							with a request to Scott to deliver it.  They 							arrived back at the Fram on the 25th of January 1912, 							just 38 days after leaving the south pole. 							The whole trip had taken 99 days, 10 less than anticipated, 							they had covered 3,440km (1,860 nautical miles), five 							men and fifty two dogs had set off, five men and eleven 							dogs had returned.
  Scott left his camp with men travelling with 							motor sledges, dogs, ponies and on foot on the 1st of 							November 1911. Only a small party of four would 							go all the way to the pole, the others were there to 							help transport food and supplies to be used on the outward 							journey and left at supply dumps to be used by the pole 							group on the way back again. The ponies were killed 							during this march, some of the meat eaten and some of 							it depoted as Amundsen did with his dogs. Eventually 							there were two teams each of four men manhauling sledges 							and provisions, though he had planned for four, Scott 							decided to take a five man party to the pole including 							himself, the remaining three turned back to the ship.							 Scott's party arrived at the pole on the 17th 							of January 1912, 77 days after setting off.   They found that Amundsen had beaten them by 							five weeks, their condition was deteriorating by this 							time due to an insufficient diet in quantity and nutrients, 							scurvy was probably beginning to take effect. They rested 							for a day and set off to return on the 19th. Despite 							unusually cold weather, their diet and low morale, they 							made good progress to the edge of the polar plateau. 							During the 100 mile descent of the Beardmore Glacier 							from the plateau down to near sea level one of the party, 							Edgar Evans suffered a fall on the 4th of February that 							left him "dull and incapable", he never really recovered 							and fell again on the 17th of February dying near to 							the foot of the glacier.   Scott had left 							orders for dog teams to meet the polar party at 82.30S 							about the 1st of March 1912, the remaining 							four men reached this point by the 27th of February. 							By the 10th of March they gave up on seeing the dogs 							arriving, they had about 400 miles left to go in difficult 							surface conditions and unusually cold temperatures. 							On this day Captain Lawrence Oates with badly frostbitten 							feet walked out from the tent in a blizzard to his death, 							his last reported words were "I am just going outside 							and may be some time". They walked another 20 miles, 							all suffering badly now and made their final camp on 							the 19th of March. Blizzards prevented further progress 							and they stay in their tent with dwindling supplies, 							Scott's final diary entry was on the 29th of March, 							
......
